title: Asthma — Chronic Management
oneLiner: Chronic airway inflammation with reversible airflow obstruction; GINA stepwise therapy prevents exacerbations and controls symptoms.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic Vignette**

A 24-year-old non-smoker presents with episodes of wheezing and chest tightness over the past year. Symptoms are worse at night, often waking them at 3 am, and occur after running in the park or when they visit a friend’s cat. Between attacks they feel completely well. On examination, you hear a mild expiratory wheeze; chest is not hyperinflated. Spirometry shows FEV1/FVC 0.68 with 15% reversibility post-salbutamol.

**Dissect the clue pattern**

| Clue in vignette | What it tells you |
|------------------|--------------------|
| Young adult, non-smoker | Low suspicion for COPD; autoimmune rare |
| Nocturnal wakening | Airway hyperresponsiveness peaks in early morning — hallmark of asthma |
| Triggered by exercise & cat allergen | Type I hypersensitivity; IgE-mediated |
| Normal between attacks | Variable airflow obstruction — diagnostic key |
| Expiratory wheeze | Narrowed airways, forced expiration amplifies turbulence |
| FEV1/FVC <0.7 with ≥12% reversibility | Confirms obstructive disease that is reversible (bronchodilator response) |

**Atypical presentations that may trick you**

1. **Cough-variant asthma** — No wheeze; only chronic dry cough, often worse at night. Easy to dismiss as post-nasal drip or GERD. Clue: cough improves with bronchodilators.
2. **Elderly asthma** — Wheeze and dyspnoea with smoking history; easily mislabelled as COPD. Clue: marked diurnal variability and eosinophilia.
3. **Occupational asthma** — Symptoms only during workdays, improve on weekends/holidays. Clue: ask “how are your symptoms when you are off work?” (isocyanates, flour, wood dust).

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-Language Definition**

Asthma is an inflammatory disease of the smaller airways. The inflammation makes the airways twitchy (hyperresponsive) and prone to squeezing, swelling, and extra mucus production. Treatment aims to quiet that inflammation and keep the airways open.

**Why it matters clinically**

- **Frequency:** ~8% of adults, 10% of children; one of the most common chronic illnesses.
- **Stakes:** Accounts for ~1400 deaths in the UK per year — many are preventable. Missed diagnosis → irreversible airway remodelling.
- **Pitfalls:** Confusing it with COPD, overusing reliever inhalers (SABA only without ICS → increased exacerbation risk), under-treating in pregnancy or perioperatively.

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The story of how asthma develops**

It starts with sensitisation: exposure to an allergen (e.g., house dust mite) in a genetically predisposed person → Th2 lymphocyte activation → production of IgE antibodies that coat mast cells. On re-exposure, the allergen cross-links IgE → mast cell degranulation → histamine, leukotrienes, prostaglandins. This causes immediate bronchoconstriction. Hours later, eosinophils and Th2 cells infiltrate the airway wall → release of cytokines (IL-4, IL-5, IL-13) → chronic inflammation, mucus gland hyperplasia, basement membrane thickening (remodelling).

**Key mechanism → sign connections**

| Pathophysiological event | Resulting clinical sign |
|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| Bronchial smooth muscle contraction | Wheeze (expiratory > inspiratory) |
| Mucosal oedema + mucous plugging | Prolonged expiration, chest tightness |
| Airway hyperresponsiveness | Diurnal PEF variability >20% |
| Eosinophilic infiltration | Elevated FeNO (fractional exhaled nitric oxide) |
| Mucus hypersecretion | Cough with clear, sticky sputum |
| Air trapping (incomplete exhalation) | Hyperinflation (barrel chest, loss of cardiac dullness) |

**Connecting pathophys to pattern recognition**

The night-time cough is explained by circadian dip in cortisol + peak inflammatory mediators at 2-4am. Exercise-induced asthma is due to hyperventilation cooling and drying the airways → mast cell activation. The “silent chest” during a severe attack is the most ominous sign — so much airway narrowing that air movement is too low to produce wheeze. That is a pre-arrest state.

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins):
| Category | Common causes | Stem clue pointing to it |
|----------|---------------|--------------------------|
| **Allergic** | Pollen, house dust mite, pet dander, mould | Symptoms seasonal or after environment change (e.g., “got worse after visiting a farm/fluffy friend’s house”) |
| **Non-allergic triggers** | Exercise, cold air, emotional stress, drugs (aspirin/NSAIDs, β-blockers) | “Wheeze only when I run” or “started after eye drops (timolol)” |
| **Infections** | Viral URTI (rhinovirus, RSV, influenza) | “Every time I get a cold, my chest tightens up” |
| **Occupational** | Isocyanates (paint), flour (bakers), wood dust, lab animals | “Symptoms better on holiday, worse Monday” |
| **Other** | GERD (acid microaspiration), menstrual cycle (hormones), food (rare) | “Asthma worse lying flat or before period” |

**Most common trigger worldwide:** Viral respiratory infections and aeroallergens (especially house dust mite).

---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria (GINA 2024)**

> Variable expiratory airflow limitation (FEV1/FVC <0.7 + ≥12% and ≥200 mL increase in FEV1 after bronchodilator or during corticosteroid therapy) **OR** peak expiratory flow (PEF) variability >20% over 2 weeks.

**Key tests**

| Test | What it shows | Why you order it | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|------------------|------------------|
| Spirometry pre/post bronchodilator | Obstructive pattern + reversibility | Gold standard for diagnosis | ΔFEV1 ≥200 mL and ≥12% = positive. Normal between attacks does not rule out |
| Peak expiratory flow (PEF) diary | Diurnal variation | Confirm variable obstruction, monitor home | Morning reading <80% of evening or variability >20% over 2 weeks |
| FeNO (fractional exhaled nitric oxide) | Eosinophilic airway inflammation | >50 ppb supports diagnosis, predicts response to ICS | Low FeNO (<25 ppb) suggests non-eosinophilic asthma or alternative diagnosis |
| Blood eosinophils | Systemic eosinophilia | ≥300 cells/µL → severe asthma, candidate for biologics | Not specific; can be normal even in eosinophilic asthma |
| Allergy testing (skin prick or IgE) | Specific sensitisation | Identify triggers for avoidance | Positive = increased likelihood but does not confirm asthma |
| Chest X-ray | Rule out mimics, complications | One view; look for hyperinflation, pneumothorax, infiltrates | Normal or hyperinflated. Not diagnostic |

**Pattern recognition: what single finding confirms / rules out**

- **Confirms:** FEV1 reversibility of ≥200 mL and ≥12% plus symptoms consistent.
- **Rules out (in an attack):** Normal spirometry during symptoms; need to do serial PEF or bronchial provocation (mannitol, methacholine). A normal FeNO does not rule out asthma.

---
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Philosophy:** Step up until control achieved, step down when stable for 3 months. Control = no daytime symptoms >2/week, no night wakening, no limitation, no exacerbations, normal PEF.

#### Immediate (first hour) — for acute exacerbation
- **Oxygen** to SpO2 ≥94% (not too high if CO2 retention suspected — rare in pure asthma)
- **Salbutamol 2.5 mg** nebulised ± ipratropium 0.5 mg (if severe: combined) — repeat every 20 min up to 3 doses
- **Prednisolone** 40–50 mg PO (or IV hydrocortisone 200 mg if unable to swallow) — **Why:** anti-inflammatory, takes 4–6h to work; vital to prevent relapse
- **IV magnesium sulfate** 1.2–2 g over 20 min (if PEF <33% predicted or not improving after first hour) — smooth muscle relaxant

#### Ward (24–72 hours)
- Continue salbutamol pMDI with spacer (4–6 puffs q4h, wean) or back to regular inhaler
- Continue prednisolone 40 mg daily for 5–7 days (no taper needed for short course)
- Start or resume ICS/LABA (e.g., beclometasone/formoterol 100/6 mcg 2 puffs BD)
- **Check inhaler technique** before discharge — single biggest “medication” error

#### Long-term (maintenance) — GINA stepwise approach

| Step | Treatment | Example (dose) |
|------|-----------|----------------|
| **1** | As-needed low-dose ICS-formoterol | Budesonide/formoterol 160/4.5 mcg one puff PRN (max 8/day) |
| **2** | Regular low-dose ICS + as-needed SABA | Beclometasone dipropionate 100 mcg BD + salbutamol PRN |
| **3** | Low-dose ICS/LABA (maintenance) | Fluticasone/salmeterol 125/25 mcg BD |
| **4** | Medium-dose ICS/LABA | Fluticasone/salmeterol 250/50 mcg BD |
| **5** | High-dose ICS/LABA + add-on LAMA ± biologics | Tiotropium 2.5 mcg once daily; omalizumab (anti-IgE) or mepolizumab (anti-IL5) |
| **6** | Oral corticosteroids (lowest dose possible) + refer to severe asthma centre | Prednisolone 5–10 mg daily |

**Dose examples inline:**
- Budesonide/formoterol 160/4.5 mcg: 1 puff as needed up to 8 puffs/day (step 1)
- Fluticasone/salmeterol 125/25 mcg: 2 puffs BD (step 3)
- Mepolizumab: 100 mg SC every 4 weeks
- Prednisolone: *do not* start long-term without specialist input

**What NOT to do (common pitfalls)**
- Do not step down too fast — wait at least 3 months of good control
- Do not give SABA monotherapy beyond step 1 without ICS — increases risk of death (SABA-only paradox)
- Do not use long-acting β-agonists (LABA) without ICS
- Do not forget to check inhaler technique — up to 80% of patients use incorrectly
- Do not give β-blockers (including timolol eye drops) — can precipitate fatal bronchospasm

---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Asthma | COPD | Vocal Cord Dysfunction | Heart Failure (“Cardiac Asthma”) |
|---------|--------|------|------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Age** | Any, often <40 | >45, smoker | Any (young female common) | >60, CAD/HTN |
| **Smoking** | Uncommon | Almost always | Not related | Related to CVD |
| **Cough** | Dry or sticky sputum | Productive, chronic | Dry, stridor-like | Frothy/pink sputum |
| **Wheeze** | Expiratory | Expiratory | **Inspiratory** predominant | Expiratory (usually bilateral crackles) |
| **Diurnal variation** | Marked (morning dip) | Minimal | None | None |
| **Reversibility** | ≥12% + 200 mL | <12% or minimal | No change | No change (BNP elevated) |
| **Chest X-ray** | Normal/hyperinflation | Hyperinflation, bullae | Normal | Cardiomegaly, pleural effusion |
| **FeNO** | Elevated (>50 ppb) | Normal/low | Normal | Normal |
| **Key differentiating clue** | Response to bronchodilator + ICS | Irreversible obstruction | Flow-volume loop shows variable extrathoracic obstruction | Crackles, JVP, raised BNP |

---
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Complication | How it presents | Key signs | What to do |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Near-fatal asthma** | Rapid deterioration over hours; silent chest; cyanosis; rising PCO2 | No wheeze (ominous), accessory muscles, paradox, SpO2 <92%, PaCO2 normal or high (the patient is tiring) | Call ICU; consider NIV or intubation; IV magnesium; **do not sedate** |
| **Pneumothorax** | Sudden pleuritic chest pain + dyspnoea in an attack | Hyperresonance, absent breath sounds, tracheal deviation if tension | Immediate chest drain; high-flow O2 |
| **Airway remodelling** | Fixed airway obstruction over years; incomplete reversibility | FEV1 declines faster; PEF variability lessens | Step up therapy; refer severe asthma; inhaled antifibrotic? (limited) |
| **Atelectasis** | Persistent cough, low-grade fever, opacified lobe after exacerbation | Dullness, bronchial breathing | Bronchodilators + physiotherapy; rarely bronchoscopy |
| **Allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis (ABPA)** | Severe asthmatic with worsening symptoms, brown mucus plugs | Central bronchiectasis, eosinophilia >1000, total IgE high | Oral itraconazole; prednisolone |

**Late-presentation patterns that test “what happens if untreated”**

- “A 20-year-old with known asthma, found at home obtunded, no wheeze, respiratory rate 6, pH 7.1, pCO2 12 kPa.” → **Respiratory arrest** from silent chest. Immediate intubation.
- “Patient with 30-year history of mild untreated asthma presents with persistent dyspnoea, FEV1 50% predicted, no reversibility.” → **Remodelling** — fixed obstruction now behaves like COPD.

---
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics**

- **DAMN CAT:** Triggers for asthma
  - Drugs (NSAIDs, β-blockers)
  - Allergens
  - Mould / dust
  - Night / exercise
  - Cold air
  - Anxiety
  - Thunderstorms (literally — pollen grains rupture)

- **S.T.E.P.** up GINA:
  - Step 1: **S**OS low-dose ICS/formoterol
  - Step 2: **T**ake regular low-dose ICS + SABA
  - Step 3: **E**levate to ICS/LABA
  - Step 4: **P**otentiate with medium/high-dose ICS/LABA

**Clinical pearls for pattern recognition**

1. **“Night-time cough is asthma until proven otherwise”** — especially in children and non-smokers.
2. **“If they wheeze only on exhalation, suspect asthma; if on inhalation, think vocal cords.”**
3. **“The patient who uses 2+ SABA canisters per month has uncontrolled asthma — up their ICS, don’t just refill.”**
4. **“Beware the silent chest** — no wheeze means no air movement; this is impending arrest.
5. **“Check inhaler technique with every patient visit — put an empty device in their hand and watch.”**
6. **“FeNO >50 ppb + peripheral eosinophils >300 = eosinophilic asthma that will respond beautifully to steroids and may qualify for biologics.”**
7. **“Do not give β-blockers to any patient with asthma — even ‘cardioselective’ ones can cause fatal bronchospasm.”**

**Common exam / ward mistakes to avoid**

- Calling every wheeze “asthma” — elderly + smoking = COPD; younger + stridor = vocal cord dysfunction.
- Using oral corticosteroids for mild exacerbations without first maximising inhaled therapy.
- Discharging a patient from the ED after nebulised salbutamol without prescribing prednisolone — relapse rate >80%.
- Forgetting that asthma medications are safe in pregnancy (uncontrolled asthma is far more dangerous to the fetus).

---
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call the senior / ICU / crash team**

| Parameter | Threshold | Action |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| **NEWS2** | ≥5 or any single parameter 3 | Escalate immediately to registrar |
| **SpO2** | <92% despite FiO2 0.4 | Call respiratory specialist |
| **PEF** | <33% predicted, or <100 L/min | Consider IV magnesium; senior review |
| **Speaking** | Unable to complete sentences | Need urgent treatment + escalation |
| **Chest signs** | Silent chest, cyanosis, confusion | **Crash call** — impending arrest |
| **Blood gas** | pH <7.35, or PaCO2 >4.6 kPa (normal or raised) | Patient tiring; need NIV or intubation |
| **Consciousness** | GCS <15 (any drop) | Immediate anaesthetic review |
| **Respiratory rate** | >25 breaths/min, or <8 | Need ventilatory support |

**Specific ICU thresholds**
- **Respiratory arrest:** Call crash team, start bag-valve-mask, prepare for intubation.
- **Require NIV (CPAP or BiPAP):** Only for hypercapnic asthma (rare) — prefer intubation if severely exhausted.
- **Ketamine** for induction of intubation in asthmatics (preserves respiratory drive, bronchodilates) — senior anaesthetist’s call.

**Take-home rule:** If a patient with asthma cannot speak in sentences, has a silent chest, or has a normal/raised pCO2 — they are in **extremis**. Do not wait for the next set of obs.

## Asthma — Chronic Management
**One-liner:** Chronic airway inflammation with reversible airflow obstruction; GINA stepwise therapy prevents exacerbations and controls symptoms.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic Vignette**

A 24-year-old non-smoker presents with episodes of wheezing and chest tightness over the past year. Symptoms are worse at night, often waking them at 3 am, and occur after running in the park or when they visit a friend’s cat. Between attacks they feel completely well. On examination, you hear a mild expiratory wheeze; chest is not hyperinflated. Spirometry shows FEV1/FVC 0.68 with 15% reversibility post-salbutamol.

| Clue in vignette | What it tells you |
|------------------|--------------------|
| Young adult, non-smoker | Low suspicion for COPD; autoimmune rare |
| Nocturnal wakening | Airway hyperresponsiveness peaks in early morning — hallmark of asthma |

| Expiratory wheeze | Narrowed airways, forced expiration amplifies turbulence |
| FEV1/FVC <0.7 with ≥12% reversibility | Confirms obstructive disease that is reversible (bronchodilator response) |

1. **Cough-variant asthma** — No wheeze; only chronic dry cough, often worse at night. Easy to dismiss as post-nasal drip or GERD. Clue: cough improves with bronchodilators.
2. **Elderly asthma** — Wheeze and dyspnoea with smoking history; easily mislabelled as COPD. Clue: marked diurnal variability and eosinophilia.
3. **Occupational asthma** — Symptoms only during workdays, improve on weekends/holidays. Clue: ask “how are your symptoms when you are off work?” (isocyanates, flour, wood dust).

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-Language Definition**

Asthma is an inflammatory disease of the smaller airways. The inflammation makes the airways twitchy (hyperresponsive) and prone to squeezing, swelling, and extra mucus production. Treatment aims to quiet that inflammation and keep the airways open.

- **Frequency:** ~8% of adults, 10% of children; one of the most common chronic illnesses.

- **Pitfalls:** Confusing it with COPD, overusing reliever inhalers (SABA only without ICS → increased exacerbation risk), under-treating in pregnancy or perioperatively.

---

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**The story of how asthma develops**

It starts with sensitisation: exposure to an allergen (e.g., house dust mite) in a genetically predisposed person → Th2 lymphocyte activation → production of IgE antibodies that coat mast cells. On re-exposure, the allergen cross-links IgE → mast cell degranulation → histamine, leukotrienes, prostaglandins. This causes immediate bronchoconstriction. Hours later, eosinophils and Th2 cells infiltrate the airway wall → release of cytokines (IL-4, IL-5, IL-13) → chronic inflammation, mucus gland hyperplasia, basement membrane thickening (remodelling).

**Key mechanism → sign connections**

| Pathophysiological event | Resulting clinical sign |
|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| Bronchial smooth muscle contraction | Wheeze (expiratory > inspiratory) |
| Mucosal oedema + mucous plugging | Prolonged expiration, chest tightness |
| Airway hyperresponsiveness | Diurnal PEF variability >20% |
| Eosinophilic infiltration | Elevated FeNO (fractional exhaled nitric oxide) |
| Mucus hypersecretion | Cough with clear, sticky sputum |
| Air trapping (incomplete exhalation) | Hyperinflation (barrel chest, loss of cardiac dullness) |

**Connecting pathophys to pattern recognition**

The night-time cough is explained by circadian dip in cortisol + peak inflammatory mediators at 2-4am. Exercise-induced asthma is due to hyperventilation cooling and drying the airways → mast cell activation. The “silent chest” during a severe attack is the most ominous sign — so much airway narrowing that air movement is too low to produce wheeze. That is a pre-arrest state.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins)

| Category | Common causes | Stem clue pointing to it |
|----------|---------------|--------------------------|
| **Allergic** | Pollen, house dust mite, pet dander, mould | Symptoms seasonal or after environment change (e.g., “got worse after visiting a farm/fluffy friend’s house”) |
| **Non-allergic triggers** | Exercise, cold air, emotional stress, drugs (aspirin/NSAIDs, β-blockers) | “Wheeze only when I run” or “started after eye drops (timolol)” |
| **Infections** | Viral URTI (rhinovirus, RSV, influenza) | “Every time I get a cold, my chest tightens up” |
| **Occupational** | Isocyanates (paint), flour (bakers), wood dust, lab animals | “Symptoms better on holiday, worse Monday” |
| **Other** | GERD (acid microaspiration), menstrual cycle (hormones), food (rare) | “Asthma worse lying flat or before period” |

**Most common trigger worldwide:** Viral respiratory infections and aeroallergens (especially house dust mite).

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria (GINA 2024)**

> Variable expiratory airflow limitation (FEV1/FVC <0.7 + ≥12% and ≥200 mL increase in FEV1 after bronchodilator or during corticosteroid therapy) **OR** peak expiratory flow (PEF) variability >20% over 2 weeks.

**Key tests**

| Test | What it shows | Why you order it | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|------------------|------------------|
| Spirometry pre/post bronchodilator | Obstructive pattern + reversibility | Gold standard for diagnosis | ΔFEV1 ≥200 mL and ≥12% = positive. Normal between attacks does not rule out |
| Peak expiratory flow (PEF) diary | Diurnal variation | Confirm variable obstruction, monitor home | Morning reading <80% of evening or variability >20% over 2 weeks |
| FeNO (fractional exhaled nitric oxide) | Eosinophilic airway inflammation | >50 ppb supports diagnosis, predicts response to ICS | Low FeNO (<25 ppb) suggests non-eosinophilic asthma or alternative diagnosis |

| Chest X-ray | Rule out mimics, complications | One view; look for hyperinflation, pneumothorax, infiltrates | Normal or hyperinflated. Not diagnostic |

- **Confirms:** FEV1 reversibility of ≥200 mL and ≥12% plus symptoms consistent.

**Philosophy:** Step up until control achieved, step down when stable for 3 months. Control = no daytime symptoms >2/week, no night wakening, no limitation, no exacerbations, normal PEF.

#### Immediate (first hour) — for acute exacerbation
- **Oxygen** to SpO2 ≥94% (not too high if CO2 retention suspected — rare in pure asthma)

- Start or resume ICS/LABA (e.g., beclometasone/formoterol 100/6 mcg 2 puffs BD)
- **Check inhaler technique** before discharge — single biggest “medication” error

#### Long-term (maintenance) — GINA stepwise approach

| Step | Treatment | Example (dose) |
|------|-----------|----------------|
| **1** | As-needed low-dose ICS-formoterol | Budesonide/formoterol 160/4.5 mcg one puff PRN (max 8/day) |
| **2** | Regular low-dose ICS + as-needed SABA | Beclometasone dipropionate 100 mcg BD + salbutamol PRN |
| **3** | Low-dose ICS/LABA (maintenance) | Fluticasone/salmeterol 125/25 mcg BD |
| **4** | Medium-dose ICS/LABA | Fluticasone/salmeterol 250/50 mcg BD |
| **5** | High-dose ICS/LABA + add-on LAMA ± biologics | Tiotropium 2.5 mcg once daily; omalizumab (anti-IgE) or mepolizumab (anti-IL5) |
| **6** | Oral corticosteroids (lowest dose possible) + refer to severe asthma centre | Prednisolone 5–10 mg daily |

- Fluticasone/salmeterol 125/25 mcg: 2 puffs BD (step 3)
- Mepolizumab: 100 mg SC every 4 weeks
- Prednisolone: *do not* start long-term without specialist input

**What NOT to do (common pitfalls)**

- Do not give SABA monotherapy beyond step 1 without ICS — increases risk of death (SABA-only paradox)
- Do not use long-acting β-agonists (LABA) without ICS

| Feature | Asthma | COPD | Vocal Cord Dysfunction | Heart Failure (“Cardiac Asthma”) |
|---------|--------|------|------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Age** | Any, often <40 | >45, smoker | Any (young female common) | >60, CAD/HTN |
| **Smoking** | Uncommon | Almost always | Not related | Related to CVD |
| **Cough** | Dry or sticky sputum | Productive, chronic | Dry, stridor-like | Frothy/pink sputum |
| **Wheeze** | Expiratory | Expiratory | **Inspiratory** predominant | Expiratory (usually bilateral crackles) |
| **Diurnal variation** | Marked (morning dip) | Minimal | None | None |

| **Key differentiating clue** | Response to bronchodilator + ICS | Irreversible obstruction | Flow-volume loop shows variable extrathoracic obstruction | Crackles, JVP, raised BNP |

| Complication | How it presents | Key signs | What to do |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------|------------|

- “Patient with 30-year history of mild untreated asthma presents with persistent dyspnoea, FEV1 50% predicted, no reversibility.” → **Remodelling** — fixed obstruction now behaves like COPD.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics**

- **DAMN CAT:** Triggers for asthma
  - Drugs (NSAIDs, β-blockers)
  - Allergens
  - Mould / dust
  - Night / exercise
  - Cold air
  - Anxiety
  - Thunderstorms (literally — pollen grains rupture)

- **S.T.E.P.** up GINA:
  - Step 1: **S**OS low-dose ICS/formoterol
  - Step 2: **T**ake regular low-dose ICS + SABA
  - Step 3: **E**levate to ICS/LABA
  - Step 4: **P**otentiate with medium/high-dose ICS/LABA

**Clinical pearls for pattern recognition**

1. **“Night-time cough is asthma until proven otherwise”** — especially in children and non-smokers.
2. **“If they wheeze only on exhalation, suspect asthma; if on inhalation, think vocal cords.”**
3. **“The patient who uses 2+ SABA canisters per month has uncontrolled asthma — up their ICS, don’t just refill.”**
4. **“Beware the silent chest** — no wheeze means no air movement; this is impending arrest.
5. **“Check inhaler technique with every patient visit — put an empty device in their hand and watch.”**
6. **“FeNO >50 ppb + peripheral eosinophils >300 = eosinophilic asthma that will respond beautifully to steroids and may qualify for biologics.”**
7. **“Do not give β-blockers to any patient with asthma — even ‘cardioselective’ ones can cause fatal bronchospasm.”**

**Common exam / ward mistakes to avoid**

- Calling every wheeze “asthma” — elderly + smoking = COPD; younger + stridor = vocal cord dysfunction.
- Using oral corticosteroids for mild exacerbations without first maximising inhaled therapy.
- Discharging a patient from the ED after nebulised salbutamol without prescribing prednisolone — relapse rate >80%.
- Forgetting that asthma medications are safe in pregnancy (uncontrolled asthma is far more dangerous to the fetus).

---

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call the senior / ICU / crash team**

| Parameter | Threshold | Action |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| **NEWS2** | ≥5 or any single parameter 3 | Escalate immediately to registrar |
| **SpO2** | <92% despite FiO2 0.4 | Call respiratory specialist |
| **PEF** | <33% predicted, or <100 L/min | Consider IV magnesium; senior review |
| **Speaking** | Unable to complete sentences | Need urgent treatment + escalation |
| **Chest signs** | Silent chest, cyanosis, confusion | **Crash call** — impending arrest |
| **Blood gas** | pH <7.35, or PaCO2 >4.6 kPa (normal or raised) | Patient tiring; need NIV or intubation |
| **Consciousness** | GCS <15 (any drop) | Immediate anaesthetic review |
| **Respiratory rate** | >25 breaths/min, or <8 | Need ventilatory support |

**Specific ICU thresholds**
- **Respiratory arrest:** Call crash team, start bag-valve-mask, prepare for intubation.
- **Require NIV (CPAP or BiPAP):** Only for hypercapnic asthma (rare) — prefer intubation if severely exhausted.
- **Ketamine** for induction of intubation in asthmatics (preserves respiratory drive, bronchodilates) — senior anaesthetist’s call.

**Take-home rule:** If a patient with asthma cannot speak in sentences, has a silent chest, or has a normal/raised pCO2 — they are in **extremis**. Do not wait for the next set of obs.
